name: Kelvin-Planck Statement (Second Law)
statement: No cyclic process can convert heat entirely into work with no other effect. A perfect heat engine (perpetual motion of the second kind) is impossible.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(heat_source, cyclic_engine) → COMPLETE(total_conversion_to_work) FAILS → entropy must increase, waste heat is unavoidable
source: Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics (Lord Kelvin, Max Planck)
known_resolutions: Use heat pumps to move rather than destroy entropy
Exploit non-equilibrium steady states for partial extraction

name: Carnot Efficiency Bound
statement: No heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs can exceed the efficiency 1 - T_cold/T_hot. This is an absolute upper bound regardless of engine design.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(hot_reservoir, cold_reservoir, engine_cycle) → COMPLETE(efficiency_above_Carnot) FAILS → reversibility requires zero entropy production, which requires infinite time
source: Carnot's theorem (Sadi Carnot, 1824)
known_resolutions: Combined cycle systems to approach limit
Thermoelectric and thermionic devices for different trade-offs

name: Clausius Inequality (Spontaneous Heat Flow)
statement: Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a colder body to a hotter body. Any refrigeration requires external work input.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(cold_body, hot_body, thermal_contact) → COMPLETE(spontaneous_reverse_flow) FAILS → entropy of isolated system cannot decrease
source: Clausius statement of the Second Law (Rudolf Clausius, 1854)
known_resolutions: Maxwell's demon thought experiments reveal information cost
Fluctuation theorems allow transient violations at nanoscale

name: Unattainability of Absolute Zero
statement: No finite sequence of thermodynamic operations can cool a system to exactly absolute zero. The Third Law makes T=0 an asymptotic limit requiring infinite steps.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(cooling_stages, finite_resources) → COMPLETE(T_equals_zero) FAILS → each successive step yields diminishing entropy reduction
source: Nernst unattainability principle / Third Law of Thermodynamics (Walther Nernst, 1906)
known_resolutions: Accept asymptotic approach as sufficient for quantum experiments

name: Landauer's Principle
statement: Erasing one bit of information must dissipate at least kT·ln(2) of energy as heat. Logically irreversible computation has a minimum thermodynamic cost.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(bit_erasure, thermal_environment) → COMPLETE(dissipation_free_erasure) FAILS → entropy of environment must increase to compensate information loss
source: Landauer's principle (Rolf Landauer, 1961)
known_resolutions: Operate near Landauer limit with slow erasure

name: Kochen-Specker Theorem
statement: In Hilbert spaces of dimension ≥ 3, it is impossible to assign definite values to all observables simultaneously in a way consistent with quantum predictions. Quantum mechanics is contextual — measurement outcomes depend on what else is measured.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(observables, noncontextual_value_assignment) → COMPLETE(consistent_global_assignment) FAILS → coloring of orthogonality graph is impossible
source: Kochen-Specker theorem (Simon Kochen, Ernst Specker, 1967)

name: No-Communication Theorem
statement: Entanglement cannot be used to transmit classical information faster than light. Local measurements on one half of an entangled pair cannot signal to the other party.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(entangled_pair, local_measurements) → COMPLETE(superluminal_signaling) FAILS → reduced density matrix is invariant under distant operations
source: No-communication theorem (Ghirardi, Rimini, Weber, 1980; Peres, Terno)
known_resolutions: Use classical channel alongside entanglement (quantum teleportation)
Accept entanglement as correlation, not communication

name: No-Broadcasting Theorem
statement: It is impossible to broadcast (create copies of the marginals of) an arbitrary mixed quantum state. Generalizes no-cloning to mixed states and multiple parties.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(mixed_state, broadcasting_channel) → COMPLETE(identical_marginals_for_all_parties) FAILS → non-commutativity of density operators prevents it
source: No-broadcasting theorem (Barnum, Caves, Fuchs, Jozsa, Schumacher, 1996)
known_resolutions: Classical states can be broadcast (commuting density matrices)

name: No-Deletion Theorem
statement: Given two copies of an arbitrary unknown quantum state, it is impossible to delete one copy against the other. Linearity and unitarity of quantum mechanics forbid it.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(two_copies, deletion_unitary) → COMPLETE(one_copy_plus_blank) FAILS → unitarity preserves inner products, deletion would violate this
source: No-deletion theorem (Pati and Braunstein, 2000)
known_resolutions: Information is conserved in quantum mechanics
Erasure to known state is possible (Landauer cost)

name: Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
statement: For any pair of conjugate observables (e.g., position and momentum), the product of their uncertainties has a fundamental lower bound: Δx·Δp ≥ ℏ/2. Simultaneous perfect knowledge of both is impossible.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(position_measurement, momentum_measurement) → COMPLETE(simultaneous_precision) FAILS → non-commuting operators cannot share eigenstates
source: Uncertainty principle (Werner Heisenberg, 1927; Kennard inequality)
known_resolutions: Squeezed states trade precision between conjugates
Quantum error correction encodes in decoherence-free subspaces

name: No-Hiding Theorem
statement: If quantum information is lost from a subsystem, it must have moved entirely to the environment/correlations — it cannot be hidden in the correlations alone without being recoverable from the environment.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(quantum_system, environment, decoherence) → COMPLETE(information_destruction) FAILS → unitarity ensures information is relocated, not destroyed
source: No-hiding theorem (Braunstein and Pati, 2007; experimentally verified by Samal et al., 2011)
known_resolutions: Quantum error correction recovers information from environment
Black hole information paradox connects to this
Decoherence is information leakage, not loss

name: Weinberg-Witten Theorem
statement: A massless particle with spin j > 1/2 cannot carry a Lorentz-covariant conserved current, and a massless particle with spin j > 1 cannot carry a Lorentz-covariant stress-energy tensor. This constrains composite gravitons and limits emergent gravity scenarios.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(massless_high_spin_particle, Lorentz_covariant_current) → COMPLETE(conserved_observable) FAILS → representation theory of Poincaré group forbids it
source: Weinberg-Witten theorem (Steven Weinberg, Edward Witten, 1980)
known_resolutions: Graviton is fundamental, not composite of charged constituents
String theory evades via extended objects
AdS/CFT emergent gravity evades by changing spacetime structure

name: Relativistic Speed Limit
statement: No massive particle can be accelerated to the speed of light. As v→c, the energy required diverges to infinity. Causal signals cannot propagate faster than c.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(massive_particle, energy_input) → COMPLETE(v_equals_c) FAILS → relativistic mass-energy diverges: E = γmc², γ→∞
source: Special relativity (Albert Einstein, 1905)
known_resolutions: Alcubierre metric as theoretical framework (requires exotic matter)
Quantum tunneling allows evanescent superluminal group velocity without information transfer

name: Penrose Singularity Theorem
statement: Given physically reasonable energy conditions, once a trapped surface forms, spacetime must contain a geodesic incompleteness (singularity). Gravitational collapse to a singularity is inevitable, not a special-case artifact.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(trapped_surface, positive_energy_condition, global_hyperbolicity) → COMPLETE(geodesic_completeness) FAILS → focusing theorem forces geodesic convergence in finite affine parameter
source: Penrose singularity theorem (Roger Penrose, 1965; Hawking-Penrose theorems, 1970)

name: Chronology Protection Conjecture
statement: The laws of physics prevent the formation of closed timelike curves (time machines) in any physically realizable spacetime. Quantum effects diverge at the chronology horizon, destroying the time machine.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(spacetime_geometry, closed_timelike_curves) → COMPLETE(stable_time_machine) FAILS → quantum vacuum fluctuations diverge at chronology horizon
source: Chronology protection conjecture (Stephen Hawking, 1992); supported by semiclassical calculations
known_resolutions: Remains a conjecture — full quantum gravity needed
Gödel and Kerr metrics are mathematical solutions but physically unrealizable

name: Cosmic Censorship Conjecture
statement: Singularities formed by gravitational collapse are generically hidden behind event horizons (weak form). The maximal Cauchy development of generic initial data is inextendible (strong form). Naked singularities are forbidden.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(gravitational_collapse, generic_initial_data) → COMPLETE(visible_singularity) FAILS → event horizon formation censors the singularity from external observers
source: Cosmic censorship conjecture (Roger Penrose, 1969); weak and strong forms

name: Bekenstein Entropy Bound
statement: The maximum entropy (information) that can be contained in a region of space is bounded by S ≤ 2πkRE/(ℏc), proportional to the surface area, not the volume. You cannot pack unlimited information into finite space.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(energy_E, region_radius_R) → COMPLETE(entropy_exceeding_bound) FAILS → exceeding the bound would form a black hole, collapsing the region
source: Bekenstein bound (Jacob Bekenstein, 1981); related to holographic principle
known_resolutions: Black hole as maximum entropy object

name: Spontaneous Ergodicity Breaking
statement: In systems undergoing phase transitions, ergodicity is spontaneously broken: the system becomes trapped in one of multiple symmetry-broken sectors and cannot explore the full phase space in any finite time. Time averages no longer equal ensemble averages.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(symmetric_Hamiltonian, thermodynamic_limit, low_temperature) → COMPLETE(ergodic_exploration) FAILS → free energy barriers between sectors grow with system size
source: Spontaneous symmetry breaking and ergodicity breaking (Landau theory; Parisi replica symmetry breaking for spin glasses)
known_resolutions: Simulated annealing to escape local minima

name: KAM Theorem (Integrability Breakdown)
statement: Most integrable Hamiltonian systems, when subjected to small perturbations, lose most of their invariant tori. Only tori with sufficiently irrational frequency ratios survive. Generic perturbation destroys complete integrability and can lead to chaos.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(integrable_system, small_perturbation) → COMPLETE(all_tori_preserved) FAILS → resonant tori are destroyed, creating chaotic regions between surviving ones
source: KAM theorem (Kolmogorov 1954, Arnold 1963, Moser 1962)
known_resolutions: Nekhoroshev estimates give exponentially long stability

name: Mermin-Wagner Theorem
statement: Continuous symmetries cannot be spontaneously broken at finite temperature in systems with sufficiently short-range interactions in dimensions d ≤ 2. No long-range order exists in 1D or 2D for continuous symmetry groups.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(continuous_symmetry, short_range_interactions, d_leq_2) → COMPLETE(spontaneous_symmetry_breaking) FAILS → infrared divergence of Goldstone mode fluctuations destroys order
source: Mermin-Wagner theorem (N. David Mermin, Herbert Wagner, 1966; Hohenberg, 1967)
known_resolutions: Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition (topological order without symmetry breaking)
Discrete symmetries can break in 2D (Ising model)
Long-range interactions can restore order in low dimensions

name: Abbe Diffraction Limit
statement: The minimum resolvable feature size of a conventional optical system is bounded by d ≈ λ/(2·NA), where λ is wavelength and NA is numerical aperture. Sub-wavelength resolution is impossible with propagating waves alone.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(propagating_light, far_field_lens) → COMPLETE(sub_wavelength_resolution) FAILS → evanescent waves carrying fine detail decay exponentially and are lost in far field
source: Abbe diffraction limit (Ernst Abbe, 1873); Rayleigh criterion

name: No Perfect Absorber (Radiation Reaction)
statement: A charged particle cannot be accelerated without radiating energy. The Abraham-Lorentz force shows that radiation reaction is unavoidable for any accelerating charge, creating fundamental limits on lossless electromagnetic manipulation.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(charged_particle, acceleration) → COMPLETE(radiation_free_motion) FAILS → Maxwell's equations require accelerating charges to radiate
source: Larmor formula (Joseph Larmor, 1897); Abraham-Lorentz force
known_resolutions: Exploit radiation for useful purposes (antennas, synchrotron sources)
Non-radiating current configurations in specific geometries (anapole modes)
Quantum electrodynamics regularizes classical self-energy divergence

name: Chu-Harrington Limit (Antenna Bandwidth)
statement: An electrically small antenna cannot simultaneously achieve high gain, wide bandwidth, and small size. The minimum Q-factor (inversely related to bandwidth) increases as (ka)^{-3} for an antenna of radius a at wavenumber k.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(small_size, wide_bandwidth, high_efficiency) → COMPLETE(all_three) FAILS → stored reactive energy dominates radiated power for small antennas
source: Chu limit (Lan Jen Chu, 1948); Wheeler and Harrington extensions
known_resolutions: Metamaterial-inspired designs to approach but not exceed limits
Accept trade-off between size and bandwidth

name: Halting Problem (Undecidability)
statement: No algorithm can decide, for every possible program-input pair, whether the program will halt or run forever. The halting function is uncomputable.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(program, input, decision_procedure) → COMPLETE(universal_halt_detector) FAILS → self-referential diagonalization creates contradiction
source: Turing's theorem (Alan Turing, 1936)
known_resolutions: Decide for restricted program classes (primitive recursive, finite automata)
Rice's theorem generalizes: all non-trivial semantic properties are undecidable

name: Rice's Theorem
statement: Every non-trivial semantic property of programs is undecidable. You cannot algorithmically determine whether an arbitrary program computes a function with any specific non-trivial property.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(arbitrary_program, semantic_property_test) → COMPLETE(decision) FAILS → reduces to halting problem via program transformation
source: Rice's theorem (Henry Gordon Rice, 1953)
known_resolutions: Type systems as partial semantic guarantees

name: Gödel's Incompleteness Theorems
statement: Any consistent formal system powerful enough to express arithmetic contains true statements that cannot be proved within the system (First). No such system can prove its own consistency (Second).
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(consistency, completeness, expressive_power) → COMPLETE(all_three) FAILS → self-referential Gödel sentence forces the choice between consistency and completeness
source: Gödel's incompleteness theorems (Kurt Gödel, 1931)
known_resolutions: Stronger systems can prove previously unprovable statements (ordinal analysis)
Computational proof assistants for verified fragments

name: Time Hierarchy Theorem
statement: Given more time, a Turing machine can solve strictly more problems. For any time-constructible function f, there exist problems solvable in O(f(n)·log(f(n))) time but not in O(f(n)) time. The complexity hierarchy is strict and infinite.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(limited_time_f(n), all_problems) → COMPLETE(solve_everything_solvable_in_more_time) FAILS → diagonalization constructs problems requiring genuinely more time
source: Time hierarchy theorem (Hartmanis and Stearns, 1965)
known_resolutions: Practical algorithms focus on constant factor improvements within a class

name: Shannon Circuit Complexity Lower Bound
statement: Most Boolean functions on n variables require circuits of size at least 2^n / n. There are not enough small circuits to compute all functions — but we cannot identify specific hard functions. Proving explicit circuit lower bounds is the central barrier in complexity theory.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(polynomial_size_circuits, all_Boolean_functions) → COMPLETE(coverage) FAILS → counting argument shows exponentially more functions than circuits, but constructive proofs remain elusive
source: Shannon counting argument (Claude Shannon, 1949); Razborov-Rudich natural proofs barrier (1997)
known_resolutions: Natural proofs barrier explains why standard techniques fail

name: FLP Impossibility (Consensus in Async Systems)
statement: In an asynchronous distributed system, no deterministic consensus protocol can guarantee agreement if even a single process may crash. The impossibility is fundamental: you cannot distinguish a crashed process from a slow one.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(asynchronous_network, deterministic_protocol, one_crash_fault) → COMPLETE(guaranteed_consensus) FAILS → bivalent initial configuration leads to infinite indecision chain
source: FLP impossibility (Fischer, Lynch, Paterson, 1985)

name: Byzantine Generals Bound
statement: In a system with n processes, Byzantine fault-tolerant consensus is impossible if the number of faulty (arbitrary-behavior) processes f ≥ n/3. You need at least 3f+1 total processes to tolerate f Byzantine faults.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(n_processes, f_Byzantine_faults, consensus_protocol) → COMPLETE(agreement_when_f_geq_n_div_3) FAILS → faulty processes can equivocate, creating irreconcilable views among honest processes
source: Byzantine generals problem (Lamport, Shostak, Pease, 1982)
known_resolutions: Digital signatures reduce bound to f < n/2
Blockchain as probabilistic BFT with proof-of-work

name: Sybil Attack Impossibility
statement: In a permissionless system without identity verification, it is impossible to prevent a single adversary from creating arbitrarily many pseudonymous identities. Voting-based protocols cannot be secure without identity.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(open_membership, pseudonymity, voting_mechanism) → COMPLETE(sybil_resistant_consensus) FAILS → adversary multiplies identities to dominate any vote
source: Sybil attack (John Douceur, 2002)

name: Shannon's Perfect Secrecy Bound
statement: For a cipher to achieve perfect secrecy (ciphertext reveals zero information about plaintext), the key must be at least as long as the message. The one-time pad is optimal but key length equals message length.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(short_key, perfect_secrecy) → COMPLETE(secure_encryption) FAILS → information-theoretic argument: fewer keys than messages means some plaintexts map to same ciphertext
source: Shannon's theorem on perfect secrecy (Claude Shannon, 1949)
known_resolutions: Computational security with shorter keys (AES, RSA)
Quantum key distribution for secure key exchange
Accept computational rather than information-theoretic security

name: Classical Key Distribution Impossibility
statement: Two parties cannot establish a shared secret over a public channel without either pre-shared secrets or computational assumptions. Information-theoretically secure key agreement from scratch over a classical public channel is impossible.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(public_channel, no_pre_shared_secret, eavesdropper) → COMPLETE(information_theoretic_key_agreement) FAILS → eavesdropper has same information as both parties
source: Follows from Shannon's theory; motivates Diffie-Hellman (computational assumption) and QKD (physics assumption)

name: Shannon Channel Capacity Theorem
statement: The maximum rate at which information can be reliably transmitted over a noisy channel is bounded by the channel capacity C = max_{p(x)} I(X;Y). Transmission above capacity guarantees errors; no coding scheme can exceed it.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(noisy_channel, coding_scheme, rate_above_C) → COMPLETE(reliable_communication) FAILS → any code exceeding capacity has error probability bounded away from zero
source: Shannon's noisy channel coding theorem (Claude Shannon, 1948)
known_resolutions: Turbo codes and LDPC codes approach capacity

name: Rate-Distortion Theorem
statement: For a given source and distortion measure, there is a minimum rate R(D) below which the source cannot be compressed while keeping expected distortion ≤ D. Lossy compression has a fundamental rate-distortion trade-off.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(source, rate_below_R(D), distortion_target_D) → COMPLETE(compression_within_distortion) FAILS → information-theoretic bound on achievable rate-distortion pairs
source: Rate-distortion theorem (Claude Shannon, 1959)
known_resolutions: Quantization and transform coding approach the bound
Perceptual distortion measures align with human perception

name: Shannon Source Coding Theorem (Entropy Bound)
statement: A source with entropy H cannot be losslessly compressed to fewer than H bits per symbol on average. Entropy is the absolute floor for lossless compression.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(source_with_entropy_H, lossless_code, rate_below_H) → COMPLETE(decodable_compression) FAILS → pigeonhole: shorter codes cannot represent all sequences uniquely
source: Shannon source coding theorem / noiseless coding theorem (Claude Shannon, 1948)
known_resolutions: Context modeling (PPM, PAQ) for adaptive entropy estimation

name: Arrow's Impossibility Theorem
statement: No ranked voting system for three or more alternatives can simultaneously satisfy unrestricted domain, Pareto efficiency, independence of irrelevant alternatives, and non-dictatorship. Every fair voting system has a fundamental flaw.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(ranked_preferences, fairness_axioms) → COMPLETE(social_welfare_function) FAILS → any aggregation satisfying three axioms must be dictatorial
source: Arrow's impossibility theorem (Kenneth Arrow, 1951)
known_resolutions: Cardinal utilities instead of ordinal rankings (approval voting)
